1: The word-event formula opens a new major section: the oracle against false prophets (Ezekiel 13:1-16) and prophetesses (Ezekiel 13:17-23). The conflict between true and false prophecy is one of the central crises of the pre-exilic and exilic periods (cf. 1 Kgs 22; Jeremiah 23:9-40; 27-28; Micah 3:5-12). Ezekiel's oracle addresses the specific false prophets operating in the exilic community and in Jerusalem who were contradicting his message of judgment.
2: 'Prophesy against the prophets of Israel who are prophesying' — the doubled verb creates emphasis. Ezekiel as the prophet sent against the prophets is a distinctive Ezekielian role (cf. Ezekiel 2:5-7 where the rebellious house defines the audience). 'Say to those who prophesy out of their own hearts (miLibbam): Hear the word of the LORD' — the false prophets 'prophesy from their own hearts': their messages originate in human desire, fear, or political alignment rather than divine commission. The contrast with Ezekiel is total: his messages are received despite being personally unwelcome.
3: 'Woe to the foolish prophets who follow their own spirit and have seen nothing' — woe (hoy, H1945, the funeral lament turned into judgment oracle) applied to prophets who claim to follow the divine Spirit but are actually following their own spirit (rucham). 'Have seen nothing' (velo ra'u) — in the context of prophetic authorization, the fundamental qualification is to have seen the divine council or received a genuine vision (cf. 1 Kings 22:19-22; Jeremiah 23:18). These prophets have no visionary authorization.
4: 'Your prophets are like foxes among ruins, O Israel' — the foxes/jackals that inhabit ruins: scavengers in desolated buildings, not defenders of intact structures. The metaphor is precise: the false prophets are like animals that thrive in the aftermath of destruction rather than preventing it. They occupy the ruins of whatever their failed prophecies create, profiting from breakdown rather than preventing it.
5: 'You have not gone up into the breaches or built up the wall for the house of Israel to stand in battle on the day of the LORD' — the military metaphor of defensive theology. The true prophet stands in the gap (peretz, H6556, breach/gap) where the wall is broken — interceding, warning, calling to repentance. Moses 'stood in the breach' (Psalms 106:23) to prevent YHWH's destroying Israel in the golden-calf incident. The false prophets have abandoned the breach while claiming to be Israel's spiritual defenders.
6: 'They see false visions and utter lying divinations, saying the LORD declares — but the LORD has not sent them; yet they expect their word to be confirmed' — the false prophet's professional stance: claiming YHWH's authorization while awaiting event-confirmation of their own words. The hope that circumstances will validate the prophecy ('they expect their word to be confirmed') is the opposite of the authentic prophet's position, who announces what is revealed regardless of whether it will be received.
7: YHWH directly challenges the false prophets: 'Have you not seen a false vision and spoken a lying divination when you say the LORD declares, even though I have not spoken?' — the rhetorical question exposes the false prophets' claim. The repetition of ne'um YHWH ('the LORD declares') as a false claim is an abuse of the most authoritative formula in prophetic speech. Claiming YHWH's speech-act for human words is the paradigmatic prophetic sin.
8: 'I am against you, declares the Lord GOD' — the hineni elecha formula directed at the false prophets as divine adversary. The same formula used against Tyre (Ezekiel 26:3), Sidon (Ezekiel 28:22), and Egypt (Ezekiel 29:3) is now deployed against Israel's own prophets. Being the target of YHWH's hineni elecha is as serious a judgment as any foreign nation receives in the OAN section.
9: 'My hand will be against the prophets who see false visions and divine lies; they will not be in the council of my people, nor be recorded in the records of the house of Israel, nor enter the land of Israel; then you will know that I am the Lord GOD' — the three-part exclusion: (1) from the assembly (sod ami, H5475 + H5971 — the intimate council of the people, the community's deliberative body); (2) from the written records of Israel (the community's genealogical registers determining membership and land rights); (3) from the land itself. The three exclusions are progressive in permanence. The land exclusion applies to both the pre-exile and restoration contexts.
10: 'Because they have led my people astray, saying Peace when there is no peace — and when someone builds a flimsy wall, they plaster it with whitewash' — the double crime: the message ('peace, peace' — the same phrase in Jeremiah 6:14; 8:11 as the signature of false prophecy) and the concealment (whitewash on a weak wall). The 'flimsy wall' (chayitz, H2434, the weak partition or wall) represents the political-theological structure the false prophets are supporting: Zedekiah's rebellion against Babylon, the alliance with Egypt, the belief that Jerusalem would stand.
11: 'Say to those who plaster with whitewash that it will fall — a flooding rain will come, great hailstones will fall, and a violent wind will burst out' — the three instruments of the wall's destruction are both literal (the forces of nature) and metaphorical (the Babylonian siege, which involved engineering operations against Jerusalem's walls including earthworks and battering rams). The flooding rain, hailstones, and stormy wind are the instruments of YHWH's theophanic judgment (cf. Ezekiel 38:22; Job 38:22-23).
12: 'When the wall falls, will it not be said to you: Where is the plaster you covered it with?' — the rhetorical humiliation of the false prophets after the event. The question is anticipated: when the wall that the false prophets declared safe collapses in the Babylonian assault, the community will demand an accounting. The false prophet's inability to answer the post-disaster question of 'where is your whitewash?' is the final exposure of the original fraud.
13: YHWH's own instruments of judgment: stormy wind, flooding rain in wrath, great hailstones in consuming fury. The divine weather-instruments are specifically directed at the wall and those who whitewashed it. The theological point: it is YHWH who tears down the false-prophet-supported wall, not merely natural forces or Babylonian military power. The Babylonian siege is the human instrument; YHWH is the divine causation.
14: 'I will tear down the wall you plastered with whitewash and bring it to the ground, so that its foundation is laid bare; when it falls, you will perish within it, and you will know that I am the LORD' — the prophets who whitewashed the wall will be inside it when it falls. The false prophets who assured Jerusalem of its security will be present for its fall, experiencing the consequence of their own fraud. The 'foundation laid bare' is a construction image: a collapsed wall exposes its inadequate foundation, which is what caused the failure.
15: 'So I will spend my wrath on the wall and on those who plastered it, and I will declare: The wall is gone and so are those who plastered it — the prophets of Israel who prophesied about Jerusalem and saw visions of peace for her when there was no peace' — the final epitaph of the false prophets. The wall and the whitewashers perish together. The characterization 'prophesied concerning Jerusalem and saw visions of peace for her' is the permanent summary of false prophecy's content: location-specific and peace-assuring. All false prophecy about Jerusalem in this period shared this character.
16: The closing summary of the false-prophet oracle: these prophets who saw shalom-visions for Jerusalem while YHWH declared no shalom. The contrast between the false prophets' message and YHWH's message (through Ezekiel) is total: they say shalom, YHWH says catastrophe; they are inside the city assuring its safety, YHWH is in Babylon declaring its fall. The community must choose whose word to trust.
17: 'Son of man, set your face against the daughters of your people who prophesy out of their own hearts': the second oracle targets prophetesses. Female prophets are attested in Israel (Miriam, Deborah, Huldah) but these women are explicitly unauthorized: 'out of their own hearts' is the same accusation as the male false prophets. The transition from male prophets to female prophetesses is structural: Ezekiel is comprehensive in his criticism of all illegitimate spiritual authority in the community.
18: 'Woe to the women who sew magic bands on all wrists and make veils for heads of every height, to hunt souls' — the magic bands (kesatot, H3704, a hapax legomenon; some connect to Akkadian kisu, a pouch used in Babylonian magic) and veils used in magical practices. The 'hunting of souls' (tzeyd nephashoth) — the practitioners are using ritual objects to capture or bind the psychic vitality of clients or targets. Babylonian magical texts (including the Maqlu and Shurpu series) describe similar binding rituals using bands and head-coverings.
19: 'You have profaned me among my people for handfuls of barley and pieces of bread' — the prophets (Ezekiel 13:19 addresses them as 'you') are desacralizing YHWH's name for trivial material gain. The fees: 'handfuls of barley and pieces of bread' — the most basic subsistence food, suggesting that the prophetesses' clients are from the lower economic stratum of the exilic community. The cheapness of the fee underscores the cheapness of the spiritual transaction: YHWH's holiness traded for barley.
20: 'I am against your magic bands with which you hunt lives like birds; I will tear them from your arms and set free the lives you hunt like birds' — the imagery shifts from wall-construction (male prophets) to bird-hunting (female prophetesses). The magic bands are the traps and nets used to hunt birds; YHWH tearing the bands from the prophetesses' arms is the act of liberating those they have trapped. The freed 'birds' are the people who had been spiritually bound by the false religious practices.
21: 'I will also tear off your veils and deliver my people from your grip; they will no longer be prey in your hands, and you will know that I am the LORD' — the liberation of the exilic community from false spiritual authority. The 'grip' or hand of the prophetesses is contrasted with YHWH's hand: YHWH's hand (yad YHWH) brings the authentic prophetic word; the prophetesses' hand binds and exploits. The recognition formula applied to the prophetesses: even they will know who YHWH is when their authority is broken.
22: 'Because with lies you have discouraged the righteous person, whom I had not grieved, and you have encouraged the wicked person not to turn from his evil way so as to preserve his life' — the social harm of false prophecy: it inverts the moral order. The righteous (tzaddik) are discouraged (their integrity is undermined); the wicked (rasha) are emboldened (told their lifestyle is acceptable). This inversion of the prophet's proper function (comfort to the afflicted, affliction to the comfortable) is the core crime of false prophecy in its social dimension.
23: 'Therefore you shall no longer see false visions or practice divination; I will deliver my people from your hands' — the cessation of the false prophets' authority is itself an act of deliverance. The community's liberation from false spiritual guidance is simultaneously the judgment of the false guides. The chapter closes with the recognition formula: 'then you will know that I am the LORD' — even the false prophetesses will be brought to acknowledge the LORD whose name they were using.
